Package Review: Potential Bugs

Source: R1.md

Date: 2026-03-21

Findings

1. High: multisimsum() drops df in one valid branch

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/multisimsum.R

Details: - In the branch where true is named or NULL, multisimsum() forwards df to simsum(). - In the branch where true is scalar or otherwise non-named, multisimsum() does not forward df. - This means coverage can change depending only on how true is encoded.

Relevant code: - Named branch forwards df. - Non-named branch omits df.

Validation: - A minimal reproduction with df = 1 returned coverage 1 when true = c(a = 0, b = 0). - The same data returned coverage 0 when true = 0.

Why tests miss it: - Existing tests only exercise named true vectors in tests/testthat/test-multisimsum.R. - There is no test for scalar true combined with df.

2. High: numeric ci.limits uses the wrong formula for becover

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/performance.R

Details: - The numeric ci.limits branch for bias-corrected coverage uses:

mean(est) >= est - lower

instead of checking whether mean(est) lies between lower and upper.

  • The adjacent branches for Wald intervals and column-based limits use the correct interval logic.

Validation: - With all estimates equal to -1 and ci.limits = c(-2, 0), cover returned 1 but becover returned 0. - The mean estimate is clearly inside the interval, so becover = 0 is incorrect.

Why tests miss it: - tests/testthat/test-ci.limits.R only checks that numeric ci.limits runs successfully. - It does not validate the numeric branch against expected values.

3. Medium: rbias is undefined when true = 0, but invalid values are returned silently

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/performance.R

Details: - Relative bias divides by the true value. - When true = 0, the code produces NaN or Inf for both the estimate and the Monte Carlo SE. - The package does not guard this case or warn the user.

Validation: - A direct call to .performance() with true = 0 produced rbias = NaN and mcse = NaN.

Why tests miss it: - The package includes tests using true = 0, but they only check that execution succeeds or plots are produced. - The Stata comparison tests explicitly exclude rbias.

4. Medium: relprec and relerror break when empirical SE is zero

Files: - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/performance.R - /Users/ellessenne/R-dev/rsimsum/R/simsum.R

Details: - relprec and relerror both divide by empse. - If a method has constant estimates, then empse = 0 and these statistics become Inf or NaN. - The Monte Carlo SE path for relprec also depends on a correlation that is undefined for constant vectors.

Validation: - With constant estimates, .performance() returned empse = 0, relprec = Inf, relerror = Inf, and relprec MCSE as NaN.

Why tests miss it: - There do not appear to be tests for zero-variance estimate vectors.

Summary

Most of the risk is concentrated in summary-statistic computation: - One definite forwarding bug in multisimsum(). - One definite formula bug in becover for numeric ci.limits. - Two robustness gaps around zero denominators that produce invalid output without warning.

Suggested Follow-up

  1. Fix the missing df forwarding in multisimsum().
  2. Correct the numeric ci.limits formula for becover.
  3. Add regression tests for:
    • scalar true with df in multisimsum()
    • numeric ci.limits correctness for becover
    • true = 0 with rbias
    • constant-estimate inputs for relprec and relerror
  4. Decide whether zero-denominator cases should return NA, warn, or error.

On this page

Developed by Alessandro Gasparini, Ian R. White.
